title: Permutations
description:
A permutation of integers 1,2,\ldots,n is called beautiful if there are no adjacent elements whose difference is 1.

Given n, construct a beautiful permutation if such a permutation exists.

Input: A single integer n.

Output: Print a beautiful permutation of integers 1,2,\ldots,n. If there are several solutions, you may print any of them. If there are no solutions, print "NO SOLUTION".

examples.explanation: Any permutation of {1,2,3} has an adjacent consecutive pair.

mcqChain.questionText: For n=1, is the permutation [1] beautiful?

options.label: Yes, there are no adjacent pairs at all.
No, because 1 is adjacent to itself.
No, because 0 and 1 would be adjacent.
It depends on n being even.

mcqChain.explanation: With a single element, there are no adjacent pairs, so the condition is satisfied.

wrongExplanations.1: An element is not adjacent to itself.
wrongExplanations.2: There is no 0 in the permutation.

mcqChain.hint: The rule only concerns differences between adjacent elements.

mcqChain.questionText: For n=2, does a beautiful permutation exist?

options.label: No, both orders have adjacent difference 1.
Only if we print both numbers twice.

mcqChain.explanation: 1 2 has |1-2|=1, and 2 1 has |2-1|=1. So no solution for n=2.

mcqChain.questionText: For n=3, does a beautiful permutation exist?

options.label: No, every permutation has an adjacent consecutive pair.

mcqChain.explanation: Every arrangement of {1,2,3} puts either (1,2) or (2,3) adjacent. So no solution.
mcqChain.hint: Focus on where 2 sits and its neighbors.

mcqChain.questionText: Is the permutation [1, 3, 2, 4] beautiful for n=4?

options.label: No, because 3 and 2 are adjacent with difference 1.
No, because 1 and 3 are adjacent with difference 1.
Yes, because 4 is at the end.

mcqChain.explanation: The pair (3,2) is adjacent and |3-2|=1, so it's not beautiful.

wrongExplanations.3: Position of 4 does not fix the (3,2) issue.

mcqChain.questionText: With n up to 10^6, is trying all permutations feasible?

options.label: Yes, because 10^6! is manageable with pruning.
No, the number of permutations grows too fast.
Yes, if we store them in memory.
It depends on whether n is prime.

mcqChain.explanation: Enumerating permutations is impossible at this scale; we need a direct construction.

wrongExplanations.0: Even tiny n like 20! is enormous.

mcqChain.questionText: What can we say about two adjacent numbers that have the same parity (both even or both odd)?
mcqChain.explanation: Differences between same-parity integers are even, so cannot be 1.

wrongExplanations.1: Same parity can't differ by exactly 1.
wrongExplanations.3: They can differ by more than 2.

mcqChain.hint: Even-even or odd-odd differences are even numbers.

mcqChain.questionText: Which arrangement idea minimizes risk of adjacent difference 1 for most pairs?

options.label: Place all even numbers first, then all odd numbers.
Alternate even and odd as much as possible.
Sort the numbers in ascending order.
Randomly shuffle until it looks good.

mcqChain.explanation: Putting evens together and odds together ensures adjacent pairs inside each group differ by ≥2.

wrongExplanations.1: Alternating risks adjacent consecutive numbers (e.g., 2 next to 1).

mcqChain.questionText: Test the idea for n=4: Is [2, 4, 1, 3] beautiful?
For n=5, consider [2, 4, 1, 3, 5]. Is it beautiful?

options.label: Yes, boundary pair 4 and 1 differ by 3.

mcqChain.explanation: Differences: 2,3,2,2. All ≥2.

wrongExplanations.2: 4 and 5 are not adjacent in this sequence.

mcqChain.hint: Check the junction between the even and odd groups.

mcqChain.questionText: For even n, the junction pair is (n, 1). What is |n - 1|, and from which even n does it guarantee ≥2?
mcqChain.explanation: For even n, last even is n; |n-1|=n-1 ≥3 when n≥4, so safe.

wrongExplanations.2: Only when n=2 is it 1, but junction isn't used then.

mcqChain.questionText: For odd n, the junction pair is (n-1, 1). What is |(n-1) - 1|, and from which odd n does it guarantee ≥2?
mcqChain.explanation: For odd n, last even is n-1; |(n-1)-1|=n-2 which is ≥3 when n≥5.

wrongExplanations.1: For n=3, n-2=1 which is not safe.

mcqChain.questionText: Based on small n and the junction analysis, for which n does no solution exist?
mcqChain.explanation: n=2 and n=3 have no solution; for n=1, print 1; for n≥4, evens then odds works.

mcqChain.questionText: Which ordering is a universal construction that works for all n ≥ 4?

options.label: All evens first, then all odds.
All odds first, then all evens.

mcqChain.explanation: Evens then odds is valid for every n≥4. Odds then evens fails at n=4 (3 next to 2).

mcqChain.questionText: Read n efficiently from standard input in Python.
mcqChain.explanation: Reading a line and stripping is efficient and robust. Others either parse incorrectly or rely on unsupported functions.

wrongExplanations.1: Works only if input has no extra tokens; less robust here.
wrongExplanations.3: raw_input() is for Python 2, not Python 3.

mcqChain.questionText: Handle the base case n == 1.
mcqChain.explanation: Print 1 when n==1. Assignment (=) is invalid in conditions and other branches are incorrect.

wrongExplanations.1: Single = is a syntax error in Python conditions.
wrongExplanations.3: We must output 1 in this case.

mcqChain.questionText: Handle the impossible cases next.
mcqChain.explanation: Only n=2 and n=3 are impossible. Including 4 is wrong; excluding 2 is wrong; doing nothing for 3 is wrong.
mcqChain.hint: From earlier: only 2 and 3 have no solution.

mcqChain.questionText: Start the construction for all remaining n (n ≥ 4). Initialize spacing control and print evens.
mcqChain.explanation: We must print all evens 2..n with correct spacing; using first avoids a trailing/leading space issue.

wrongExplanations.1: range(2, n, 2) misses n when n is even and leaves trailing spaces.
wrongExplanations.3: Step 3 skips many evens and first=False would prepend a space incorrectly later.

mcqChain.questionText: Now print the odds after the evens, continuing spacing logic.

options.label:
for x in range(1, n, 2):
        sys.stdout.write(' ' + str(x))

for x in range(1, n+1, 2):
        print(x)

for x in range(1, n+2, 2):
        sys.stdout.write(str(x))

mcqChain.explanation: Use the same spacing rule and range(1, n+1, 2) to include n if odd.

wrongExplanations.1: Always adds a leading space; also misses n if odd due to stop n.
wrongExplanations.3: range(1, n+2, 2) overshoots when n is odd.

mcqChain.hint: Mirror the even loop but start at 1.

mcqChain.questionText: Finish by ending the line.
mcqChain.explanation: Write a newline at the end. Flushing or carriage return is not a substitute for newline.

wrongExplanations.1: Would print a newline regardless of branch alignment; less consistent with our streaming.

mcqChain.hint: Terminate the output with a newline character.

mcqChain.questionText: If n = 4, what does the program print?
mcqChain.explanation: Evens then odds gives 2 4 1 3.

mcqChain.questionText: If n = 3, what does the program print?
mcqChain.explanation: The branch for n in (2,3) prints NO SOLUTION.

mcqChain.questionText: What is the time complexity of this construction?
mcqChain.explanation: We loop through each number once in the evens and odds ranges.

wrongExplanations.2: We construct n outputs, so not constant time.

mcqChain.hint: Count how many iterations are executed.

mcqChain.questionText: What is the additional space complexity (excluding the output itself)?
mcqChain.explanation: We use constant extra space (a few variables) and stream output.

wrongExplanations.1: We don't store the whole sequence in a list.

mcqChain.hint: We aren't building arrays; we write as we go.

mcqChain.questionText: Why is n=3 impossible in one sentence?

options.label: Because 2 must be adjacent to either 1 or 3 in any arrangement.
Because 1 must be at the start.
Because 2 and 3 cannot both be in the permutation.

mcqChain.explanation: Wherever 2 is placed, at least one neighbor is consecutive (1 or 3).

wrongExplanations.1: Parity alone doesn't forbid a solution for larger odd n.

mcqChain.hint: Consider the position of 2 and its neighbors.

reasoningChain.question: What happens for very small n (1,2,3)?
reasoningChain.insight: Test tiny cases to see that n=1 is solvable but n=2 and n=3 are not.

reasoningChain.question: How can we avoid adjacent difference 1?
reasoningChain.insight: Adjacent numbers of the same parity never differ by 1.

reasoningChain.question: What arrangement uses that property effectively?
reasoningChain.insight: Place all evens first, then all odds; verify the boundary pair is safe for n≥4.

reasoningChain.question: Which n are impossible and which general construction works?
reasoningChain.insight: Only n=2 and n=3 are impossible; evens then odds works for all n≥4.

reasoningChain.question: How to implement efficiently in Python?
reasoningChain.insight: Read input, handle base/impossible cases, then stream-print evens and odds with correct spacing.

reasoningChain.question: Does it meet constraints?
reasoningChain.insight: Time is O(n) and extra space is O(1) beyond output; fits limits up to 10^6.
